title: Alex The Gofer

jokes.text: Like in No Strings, where they eliminated the violins and put the brass and the woodwinds right on stage, whereas in Man of La Mancha, they put them backstage, separated between the two wings. And of course, there was that wonderful way that Andrei Serban orchestrated the instruments and the human voices placed within the set in his Greek Trilogy at the La MaMa theater.
jokes.setup: Producer asks a simple cabbie if he noticed where music came from in theater
jokes.punchline: Alex responds with incredibly detailed, pretentious theater knowledge
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment revealing Alex's depth and contradictions. The contrast between 'simple cabbie' and encyclopedic theater knowledge is expertly crafted.

jokes.text: Uh, are you an actor?
jokes.setup: Alex just delivered incredibly sophisticated theater analysis
jokes.punchline: Producer's stunned reaction/question reveals his shock
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective reaction beat that caps the previous joke perfectly. Economy of words amplifies the impact.

jokes.text: Well, wait a minute. I like to think a man is never too old to be a flunky.
jokes.setup: Producer says Alex is too old to be a gofer
jokes.punchline: Alex reframes being a flunky as an aspirational goal
jokes.explanation: Brilliant inversion of expectations. The word choice 'flunky' and Alex's earnest delivery make this memorably funny.

jokes.text: Something terrible has just happened. What?! I've forgotten why I'm doing this.
jokes.setup: Jim is asked to feel Latka's head for fever
jokes.punchline: Jim suddenly has existential crisis about touching another man's head
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim Ignatowski moment - his spaced-out character having a philosophical moment about the simplest task. Classic absurdist comedy.

jokes.text: I don't know. Now, what are you worried about, Latka? This is America. We got a thing here called sick pay. Oh! Oh, good, good. You pay me ten bucks, you can go home.
jokes.setup: Latka asks for sick day, Louie mentions 'sick pay' benevolently
jokes.punchline: Louie reveals sick pay means Latka pays HIM to stay home
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie - using bureaucratic language to disguise his cruelty. The misdirection is perfectly crafted and true to character.

jokes.text: You're gonna take aspirin and go right to bed with a pumpkin.
jokes.setup: Simka giving conventional medical advice to sick Latka
jokes.punchline: Adds bizarre folk remedy about sleeping with pumpkins
jokes.explanation: Perfectly sets up Jim's extended response. The casualness of the absurd advice makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I used to live on a commune, and although those who slept with pumpkins did, in fact, seem to be healthier... so did those that slept with many other vegetables.
jokes.setup: Simka suggests sleeping with pumpkins for health
jokes.punchline: Jim delivers pseudo-scientific analysis of vegetable sleeping partners
jokes.explanation: Jim's deadpan delivery of complete nonsense treated as scientific observation. The extended pause structure builds the absurdity perfectly.

jokes.text: How do you tell 'em apart? They're numbered.
jokes.setup: Tony references Rocky III as greatest since Rocky I
jokes.punchline: Alex's matter-of-fact observation about movie numbering
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective deflation of Tony's enthusiasm. Perfect economy - just two words deliver the punchline.

jokes.text: Yeah, stop me before I make another one, please.
jokes.setup: Tony asks about Rocky III's motto
jokes.punchline: Alex suggests the motto should be about stopping the sequels
jokes.explanation: Nice meta-commentary on sequel fatigue. The 'please' adds a pleading quality that enhances the humor.

jokes.text: Don't say another word. Please, don't take the fun out of this for me. Louie... Basically, I'm a sportsman.
jokes.setup: Everyone insists Louie must never find out about Alex's job
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately reveals he knows and calls himself a 'sportsman'
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Louie's predatory nature disguised as fair play. The 'sportsman' self-description is deliciously ironic.

jokes.text: I give myself five minutes.
jokes.setup: Louie knows Alex's secret and calls himself a sportsman
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's betting on how quickly he can break the secret
jokes.explanation: Brilliant payoff to the 'sportsman' setup. Shows Louie's confidence and sadistic nature in five perfect words.

jokes.text: Where is Tony taking Alex?
jokes.setup: Louie sets up game where Iggy can ask him anything
jokes.punchline: Iggy immediately asks the one thing Louie wants to know
jokes.explanation: Perfect subversion of Louie's manipulation attempt. Iggy's innocent directness defeats Louie's scheming.

jokes.text: Louie's entire game setup with Iggy to extract Alex's location
jokes.setup: Louie proposes innocent question-and-answer game
jokes.punchline: Immediately uses it to manipulate Iggy into revealing information
jokes.explanation: Showcases Louie's cunning manipulation skills. The 'game' premise is a perfect setup for exploitation.

jokes.text: Because this really feels right, so, uh, I guess that means you're gonna give me the job, right? Because, otherwise, I'd be nervous, huh?
jokes.setup: Alex interviews for gofer job with false confidence
jokes.punchline: His logic reveals underlying desperation and anxiety
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Alex's vulnerability through flawed reasoning. The nervous energy comes through clearly.

jokes.text: You don't want to give me the job because you're afraid you'll feel kind of awkward bossing around a guy who's older than you, right? No. No, we like that.
jokes.setup: Alex assumes ageism is the issue
jokes.punchline: Producers reveal they actually enjoy bossing older people around
jokes.explanation: Dark but effective reversal. Shows the producers' callousness while subverting Alex's expectations.

jokes.text: Oh, I get it. You're kidding. Yeah, sort of wry? Huh? A little playful? Broadway hip, huh? Tender, but tough?
jokes.setup: Producers make cruel comment about liking to boss older people
jokes.punchline: Alex desperately tries to reframe it as sophisticated humor
jokes.explanation: Painful and funny - Alex's self-deception to avoid confronting the truth. The list of adjectives builds the desperation.

jokes.text: Oh, right... yes... lunch.
jokes.setup: Producers ask about lunch after Alex says he already ate
jokes.punchline: Alex realizes he's supposed to GET them lunch, not discuss eating it
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective moment of dawning realization. The pause structure sells the embarrassment.

jokes.text: But what really rankles me is having great ideas and not even be able to say 'em out loud.
jokes.setup: Alex complains about various humiliations at theater job
jokes.punchline: Reveals the real frustration is intellectual suppression
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alex moment - shows his intellectual pride is what really hurts, not the physical labor.

jokes.text: Well, the other reason I kept quiet was because I-I didn't have a really great idea.
jokes.setup: Alex complains he can't share his great ideas
jokes.punchline: Immediately admits he doesn't actually have any great ideas
jokes.explanation: Excellent self-deflating moment. Alex's honesty undercuts his previous complaint perfectly.

jokes.text: Because I, uh, I've formed certain attachments that are very real to me here.
jokes.setup: Louie asks why Alex won't quit cab job for theater
jokes.punchline: Alex's overly formal way of saying he likes his coworkers
jokes.explanation: Shows Alex's emotional awkwardness - can't just say he cares about his friends.

jokes.text: I always knew that you would be the first guy to dig himself out of this slime.
jokes.setup: Louie congratulates Alex on his theater job
jokes.punchline: Describes the garage as 'slime' while congratulating escape from it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie - manages to insult everyone while seeming congratulatory. 'Slime' is a great word choice.

jokes.text: He must have the cruddiest job in the world!
jokes.setup: Alex leaves after describing his theater job euphemistically
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately sees through Alex's spin to the truth
jokes.explanation: Louie's perceptiveness cutting through Alex's self-deception. Sets up his motivation to witness Alex's humiliation.

jokes.text: I'll tell ya because... by my telling you, I'm giving you the chance to turn your life around and become a decent man.
jokes.setup: Iggy agrees to tell Louie where Alex works
jokes.punchline: Frames enabling Louie's cruelty as a chance for moral redemption
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reverse psychology from Jim. His naive optimism about Louie's potential for good is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: I'm betting that when I tell you, you won't go. I'm betting you're about to be saved.
jokes.setup: Iggy explains his moral gambit to reform Louie
jokes.punchline: Actually believes Louie might choose decency over cruelty
jokes.explanation: Jim's sincere belief in human goodness, even Louie's, is both touching and funny. Sets up the inevitable disappointment.

jokes.text: Go celebrate your newfound virtue! Another sinner saved! Hallelujah!
jokes.setup: Louie immediately leaves to torment Alex after getting the information
jokes.punchline: Jim celebrates as if his plan worked, completely missing reality
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim moment - his complete disconnect from reality is both endearing and hilarious. The religious language adds to the absurdity.

jokes.text: Look, all they have to do is go to a side set where nobody can hear them but they can be seen. You know, like in Death of a Salesman.
jokes.setup: Alex offers staging suggestion despite being told to stay quiet
jokes.punchline: References high-art theater to solve their problem
jokes.explanation: Shows Alex can't help himself from showing off his theater knowledge, even when it's unwelcome.

jokes.text: Alex, I don't want to seem rude, but we'll save the living theater. You take care of the patty melts.
jokes.setup: Alex offers sophisticated staging advice
jokes.punchline: Producer dismisses him with condescending food service reference
jokes.explanation: Brutal put-down that reduces Alex to food service while claiming not to be rude. The contrast between 'living theater' and 'patty melts' is sharp.

jokes.text: Because, this time, my skin cries out for your touch. Your mouth. Your lips. Your chest.
jokes.setup: Romantic scene continues getting more intense
jokes.punchline: Dialogue becomes increasingly passionate and physical
jokes.explanation: Escalates the discomfort by making the dialogue more explicit. Sets up the inevitable awkward moment.

jokes.text: What are you doing?! What is with this guy?
jokes.setup: Alex and actress run increasingly passionate romantic scene
jokes.punchline: Alex apparently gets too into it, shocking the actress
jokes.explanation: Payoff to the romantic scene setup. The visual comedy of whatever Alex did would be the real punchline.

jokes.text: You know, I bet I took my watch off and left it on the table and you put it in with the rest of the garbage.
jokes.setup: Producer casually mentions missing watch
jokes.punchline: Suggests Alex threw it away with garbage
jokes.explanation: Shows the casual disrespect Alex faces, but not particularly clever or original.

jokes.text: I will never tell anybody about what I saw.
jokes.setup: Louie witnesses Alex's humiliation at theater
jokes.punchline: Claims he won't tell anyone, which is obviously a lie
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie - false sympathy while savoring Alex's humiliation. We know he'll tell everyone.

jokes.text: You knew you were nothing and you were great at it.
jokes.setup: Louie explains what made Alex special
jokes.punchline: Being 'great at being nothing' as a compliment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant paradoxical observation that's both cruel and insightful. Perfectly crafted line that captures the show's philosophy about accepting yourself.

jokes.text: Hey, hey, Reiger, it's like if you got a nose job. I mean, you'd be walking around with this little nose saying, 'Hi, I'm Alex Reiger.' And everybody would say, 'That's cute, but we want the real Reiger, the one with the honker down to here.'
jokes.setup: Louie explains why Alex's job change is wrong
jokes.punchline: Extended metaphor about Alex's prominent nose
jokes.explanation: Extended metaphor that's both insulting and philosophically interesting. The physical comedy of Louie indicating nose size adds to the humor.

jokes.text: Alex, you're an errand boy. Yeah? Well, not all the time. That's true. Sometimes you're a trash compactor!
jokes.setup: Louie calls Alex an errand boy, Alex protests it's not always true
jokes.punchline: Louie agrees and gives an even more degrading alternative
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation structure where agreement makes things worse. 'Trash compactor' is a brutally funny way to describe handling garbage.

jokes.text: Ooh! Egg salad!
jokes.setup: Louie suggests Alex shove sandwich in producer's face
jokes.punchline: Louie's enthusiasm for the egg salad choice specifically
jokes.explanation: Louie's gleeful appreciation for the messiest sandwich option shows his sadistic attention to detail.

jokes.text: Now, if you'll excuse me, Louie, I'm wanted out front.
jokes.setup: Alex has been humiliated by revelation of his menial tasks
jokes.punchline: Tries to maintain dignity by emphasizing he's 'wanted'
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Alex clinging to any shred of importance despite obvious reality. The formality of 'if you'll excuse me' adds to the desperation.

jokes.text: Oh, excuse me, would you run lines with me, please?
jokes.setup: Alex just declared his importance to the production
jokes.punchline: Immediately asked to do the same humiliating task as before
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflating callback that undercuts Alex's attempt at dignity. The timing makes it brutally effective.

tmdb_overview: Alex, who loves the theater, takes a part time job as a gofer for a young producer and director, but soon feels mistreated by his younger bosses. Louie, spying on Alex at his new job, spots Alex being humiliated and uncharacteristically does not make fun of him; rather, he tries to convince Alex to stand up for himself and tell the director off. Alex is considering this when the director suddenly has a change of heart and apologizes for mistreating Alex and ignoring one of his earlier suggestions. As a result, Alex regains his dignity.
insight: Alex's promotion scam sustains 86-point comedy through relentless character-driven irony.
